About LCZ4r

Source: vignettes/about.Rmd
about.Rmd

The Project

LCZ4r is an open-source R package designed to facilitate the retrieval, analysis, and visualization of Local Climate Zone (LCZ) data for urban climate research. The package integrates with the Global LCZ Map and provides a comprehensive set of functions for mapping urban morphology, analyzing Urban Heat Island (UHI) intensity, and modeling spatiotemporal air temperature patterns.

LCZ4r was developed to lower the technical barriers for researchers, students, and practitioners working in urban climatology, urban planning, and environmental science. By providing a reproducible, well-documented workflow in R, the package enables users to go from raw LCZ data to publication-ready maps and analyses in a few lines of code.

The package also integrates with QGIS through the LCZ4r-QGIS Plugin, making its capabilities accessible to users who prefer a graphical interface.

Key Capabilities

  • LCZ Map Retrieval: Download LCZ maps for any city in the world directly from the WUDAPT global database.
  • Urban Heat Island Analysis: Quantify UHI intensity by LCZ class using meteorological station data.
  • Air Temperature Modeling: Interpolate and map air temperature across urban areas using LCZ-based spatial modeling.
  • Anomaly Detection: Identify and visualize temperature anomalies relative to LCZ baselines.
  • QGIS Integration: Run all analyses directly within QGIS via the multilingual LCZ4r-QGIS Plugin.

The Team

LCZ4r was created and is maintained by Max Anjos and collaborators from the fields of urban climatology, geography, and data science. The project is open to community contributions — if you would like to contribute, please open an issue or pull request on GitHub.

Citation

If you use LCZ4r in your research, please cite:

Anjos, M., & Meier, F. (2024). LCZ4r: An R package for Local Climate Zone analysis and Urban Heat Island research. Nature Cities. https://doi.org/10.1038/s44284-024-00135-4

Funding and Support

The development of LCZ4r has been supported by research institutions and funding agencies committed to advancing urban climate science. We are grateful to all contributors and users who have helped improve the package through feedback, bug reports, and feature requests.

On this page

Developed by Max Anjos.
